html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 挂冠自昔曾骑虎 - Metaphor for taking a bold and dangerous step in the past.
  • 闭户于今好画龙 - Metaphor for being absorbed in artistic pursuits, possibly alluding to the story of Zhang Sengyou.
  • 难得糊涂 - Philosophy of embracing simplicity and not worrying too much about details.
  • 胜事长新，年华暗老 - Metaphorical expression suggesting that while good things continue to develop, time passes unnoticed, leading to aging.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乾隆壬子 - Year 1772 during the reign of Emperor Qianlong of the Qing dynasty.
  • 楚北江夏 - Historical county in present-day Wuhan, Hubei province.
  • 诖误戍温 - Being implicated in a mistake or wrongdoing, and being exiled to Wenzhou.
  • 曾衍东 - Historical figure known for his literary and artistic talents.
  • 郑板桥 - Famous Qing dynasty artist and poet known for his calligraphy and painting, particularly bamboo and orchids.

Proper Nouns

  • 曾衍东 - Name of the person described in the text.
  • 郑板桥 - Name of the historical figure who is quoted in the text.
  • 嘉祥 - Place name, referring to the hometown of 曾衍东.
  • 温 - Refers to Wenzhou, the place where 曾衍东 was exiled.
  • 《小豆棚》 - Title of one of 曾衍东's works.
  • 《哑然集》 - Title of another work by 曾衍东.
  • 彭左海 - Name of the author of the preface, who wrote in 1806 (嘉庆丙子).

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 余家有《豆棚闲话》一编 - Literal phrase meaning 'I have a collection of *Dou Peng Xian Hua* at home.'
  • 爱其自出机杼，成一家言 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'I love it for its originality and the fact that it forms its own school of thought.'
  • 暇时尝玩适之 - Colloquial phrase meaning 'I often read and enjoy it in my leisure time.'
  • 客有谈及曾七如居士所撰《小豆棚闲话》 - Narrative phrase meaning 'a guest mentioned the *Xiao Dou Peng Xian Hua* written by Master Zeng Qiru.'
  • 其义类颇相似 - Comparative phrase meaning 'its meaning and style are quite similar.'
  • 笔墨豪放不羁 - Stylistic phrase meaning 'his brushwork is unrestrained and free-spirited.'
  • 博采旁搜，辑成一部 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'he gathered and compiled these into a book.'
  • 十余万言，奇奇怪怪，若无关于世故者 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'it consists of over ten thousand words, full of strange and unusual stories, as if they have nothing to do with worldly affairs.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《豆棚闲话》 - A book title, likely a work of literature or commentary.
  • 《小豆棚闲话》 - Another book title, a work by Zeng Qiru.
  • 东鲁嘉祥 - Jiaxiang in Donglu (eastern Lu), a place in Shandong Province.
  • 乾隆壬子 - The year Renzi during the Qianlong era (1762).
  • 楚北江夏 - Jiangxia in northern Chu, a region in modern-day Hubei Province.
  • 诖吏议戍温 - A reference to being implicated in a case and exiled to Wen, a place in Zhejiang Province.
  • 野史家钞本 - Family copies of wild histories, which are unofficial historical records.
  • 座客谈论 - Conversations with guests, often referring to intellectual or literary discussions.

Proper Nouns

  • 《豆棚闲话》 - The title of the book the author has.
  • 《小豆棚闲话》 - The title of the book by Zeng Qiru.
  • 曾七如居士 - Zeng Qiru, a scholar and writer.
  • 东鲁嘉祥 - Jiaxiang in Donglu (eastern Lu).
  • 乾隆壬子 - The year 1762.
  • 楚北江夏 - Jiangxia in northern Chu.
  • 温 - Wen, a place in Zhejiang Province.
  • 野史家钞本 - Family copies of wild histories.
  • 座客谈论 - Conversations with guests.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 其自目为“闲书” - He (or they) call it 'idle book'
  • 层见叠出 - Recurring and layered occurrences
  • 诠次成帙 - Classify and compile into volumes
  • 花晨月夕 - Flower-filled mornings and moonlit evenings
  • 腾贵鸡林 - Rise in value in Goryeo (Korea)
  • 略识七如氏之梗概 - Briefly understand the outline of the Seven Li Family

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 闲书 - Refers to a type of book that is not considered serious or scholarly, often associated with entertainment or leisure reading
  • 花晨月夕 - A classical phrase referring to beautiful natural scenes, often used in literature to describe ideal moments for reading or contemplation
  • 腾贵鸡林 - 'Chicken Forest' is a poetic name for Korea, suggesting the spread of the book's fame or popularity in that region
  • 七如氏 - Likely refers to a family or group associated with the author or the text, possibly a pseudonym or a reference to a specific literary tradition
  • 光绪六年岁次庚辰 - The year 1880 in the reign of Emperor Guangxu, using the Chinese sexagenary cycle (庚辰 is the 41st year in the cycle)
  • 孟春月 - The first month of spring in the Chinese lunar calendar
  • 东垣 - A place name, possibly referring to the author's hometown or a specific location

Proper Nouns

  • 腾贵鸡林 - A poetic name for Korea
  • 七如氏 - A family or group name, possibly a pseudonym
  • 光绪六年岁次庚辰 - The year 1880 in the reign of Emperor Guangxu
  • 孟春月 - The first month of spring in the Chinese lunar calendar
  • 东垣 - A place name, possibly the author's hometown

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 作此等书，必其人闲、其所遭之时闲、其所处之境闲，而后能以闲心情为闲笔墨 - A series of conditional statements emphasizing the need for leisure, time, and environment to write an idle book.
  • 忙里偷闲 - An idiom meaning 'to find time for leisure in the midst of busyness.'
  • 野史家抄本剩录 - Literally 'leftover copies of wild history books,' referring to obscure or unofficial historical records.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秀才 - A scholar who has passed the lowest level of the imperial examinations, often associated with the lower ranks of the literati.
  • 幕 - Refers to working as an official in a government office or as a retainer in a noble household.
  • 客 - Refers to traveling or working as a guest in someone else's household, often in a menial capacity.
  • 山川古迹 - Refers to natural landscapes and historical sites, often associated with cultural or spiritual significance.
  • 人事怪异 - Refers to unusual or extraordinary human events or phenomena, often associated with folklore or supernatural tales.

Proper Nouns

  • 《小豆棚》 - The title of the book being discussed, likely a collection of stories or anecdotes.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 信笔直书 - Write freely; describes spontaneous writing in a busy and noisy environment.
  • 转觉闲而且闲 - Turn to feel increasingly idle; describes a shift in perception.
  • 化出 - Arise from; describes the transformation of busyness into leisure.
  • 贵心闲 - Precious to have a leisurely mind; emphasizes the importance of mental leisure.
  • 无往不得其闲 - Everywhere one goes, one finds leisure; describes the pervasive nature of mental leisure.
  • 不与之逐而与之适 - Not chasing after them but adapting to them; describes a state of mental equanimity.
  • 竟成 - Transformed into; describes the transformation of idle elements into a literary work.
  • 谀余者 - Flatter me; describes a flattering statement.
  • 唯唯否吾 - Nod and shake my head, disagreeing; describes a polite but firm disagreement.
  • 诮余者 - Mock me; describes a mocking statement.
  • 唯唯否否 - Nod and shake my head, disagreeing; describes a polite but firm disagreement.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 七如 - Pen name or pseudonym of the author; may have cultural or personal significance.
  • 乾隆六十年岁在乙卯九月 - Year 1795 in the Qianlong era of the Qing dynasty, corresponding to the lunar year of the Rabbit (乙卯).
  • 曾衍东 - Real name of the author; may have historical or literary significance.

Proper Nouns

  • 七如 - Pen name or pseudonym of the author.
  • 曾衍东 - Real name of the author.
  • 乾隆六十年岁在乙卯九月 - Date of the text, corresponding to the year 1795 in the Qianlong era of the Qing dynasty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 忠孝部 - Focuses on loyalty and filial piety, central values in Confucian ethics.

  • 义勇部 - Focuses on bravery and martial valor, reflecting the importance of heroism in Chinese history.
  • 侠 - Refers to chivalrous or heroic figures, often associated with justice and righteousness.
  • 报应部 - Focuses on karma and retribution, reflecting the belief in cause and effect in Chinese philosophy.
  • 善恶并附 - Refers to stories that highlight both good and evil deeds, emphasizing moral lessons.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卷 - A 'volume' or 'book,' used in classical Chinese to denote a section of a larger work or compilation.
  • 部 - A 'section' or 'category,' used to classify the contents of a volume.
  • 祈夢事徵 - Literally 'records of dream prayers and omens,' likely referring to accounts of people praying for dreams and the omens or results that followed.
  • 青陽 - A classical name for spring or a specific place (possibly a garden or location), but could also be a title or person.
  • 玉鉤形 - 'Jade hook shape,' possibly a metaphor or a description of an object or symbol.
  • 亦暢園芝鶴小辨 - 'A small debate on the orchid and crane in the Yichang Garden,' likely a commentary or analysis on a specific motif or imagery.
  • 瓮走 - A term that could refer to a person or a type of behavior; '瓮' is a jar or pot, and '走' is to run or move.
  • 文酒 - A term often used to refer to literary gatherings or the combination of literature and alcohol.
  • 夢花記摘略 - 'A brief excerpt from *The Dream of Flowers*,' likely a literary work or story.
  • 鐘子慕 - A proper name, possibly of a person or author.
  • 十八娘外傳 - 'The *Biography of Eighteenth Lady*,' likely a fictional or historical narrative.
  • 郧阳太守俭约文 - 'A discourse on frugality by the governor of Yunyang,' a political or moral essay.
  • 奸淫變相判 - 'A judgment on the disguised forms of adultery or indecency,' likely a legal or moral commentary.
  • 曹月帆 - A proper name, possibly of a person or author.
  • 討蜘蛛網檄 - 'A proclamation against the spider's web,' metaphorical or symbolic, possibly a political or satirical text.
  • 種痘說 - 'A treatise on smallpox vaccination,' a medical or health-related text.
  • 秦桧墓詩 - 'A poem about the tomb of Qin Hui,' likely a historical or political commentary.
  • 寺壁詩 - 'Poems on temple walls,' likely a collection of inscriptions or poetry found in temples.
  • 上寮翁焙鴨論 - 'A discussion by Old Master Shangliao on roasting duck,' possibly a culinary or humorous text.
  • 骨種羊考 - 'A study on bone-seeded sheep,' possibly an agricultural or zoological treatise.
  • 賈凫西鼓詞 - 'The *Drum Words* of Jia Fuxi,' likely a form of folk or narrative poetry.
  • 再來人 - 'A person who comes again,' possibly a term for a reincarnated or spiritually returned individual.
  • 了拳 - A term possibly referring to a person or a practice; '了' can mean 'to complete' or 'to finish,' and '拳' is 'fist' or 'martial arts.'

  • 幽宮詩 - 'A poem on the幽宫 (Mysterious Palace),' possibly a symbolic or mystical reference.
  • 喜娘 - A term for a professional matchmaker or woman who sings at weddings.
  • 胳瘩老娘 - 'The Lady of the Lump,' possibly a colloquial or humorous term for a woman with a physical trait or nickname.
  • 二妙 - 'The Two Marvels,' possibly a title or reference to two notable individuals or things.
  • 顛當 - 'The Toppler,' possibly a nickname or title for someone who is clumsy or unconventional.
  • 紫歡 - 'Purple Joy,' possibly a name or a poetic reference.
  • 阿嫱 - 'A Qiang,' possibly a name or a reference to a woman.

  • 醋姑娘 - 'The Vinegar Girl,' possibly a nickname or a character known for jealousy.

  • 張睢陽 - 'Master Zhang of Suizheng,' likely a reference to Zhang Xun, a historical figure.

  • 湘潭社神 - 'The Village God of Xiangtan,' a local deity or spirit.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 卷四 祥瑞部 - Volume 4, 'Department of Auspicious Signs' – Likely a section of a larger work that records omens, miracles, or supernatural events considered auspicious.
  • 祈夢事徵 - Indicates the collection includes accounts of people praying through dreams and the results or interpretations of those dreams.
  • 青陽 - Could refer to a garden or a location associated with spring, or it may be a person's name or title.

  • 種痘說 - A medical or health-related text.
  • 秦桧墓詩 - A historical or political commentary.
  • 寺壁詩 - Likely a collection of inscriptions or poetry found in temples.
  • 上寮翁焙鴨論 - Possibly a humorous or culinary text.
  • 骨種羊考 - Possibly an agricultural or zoological treatise.
  • 賈凫西鼓詞 - Likely a form of folk or narrative poetry.
  • 再來人 - Possibly a term for a reincarnated or spiritually returned individual.
  • 了拳 - Possibly a martial arts term or a metaphor.
  • 懊上人 - Possibly a title or nickname for a monk or scholar.
  • 禹城道人 - A title for a Daoist practitioner or teacher.

  • 无殍丐 - Means 'there are no beggars or starving people.' '殍丐' refers to beggars and starving people.
  • 粟烂鸡黄 - Describes the abundance of grain and chickens. '粟烂' means 'grain is abundant,' and '鸡黄' means 'chickens are abundant.'
  • 且多豫人为贾 - Means 'and there are many people from Yu (Henan) who are merchants.' '豫人' refers to people from Henan, and '贾' means 'merchant.'
  • 询厥由来 - Means 'to inquire about the reason.' '询' means 'to inquire,' and '厥由来' means 'the reason.'
  • 吾乡人可屈指 - Means 'my fellow villagers can be counted on one hand.' '屈指' is an idiom meaning 'to count on one hand.'
  • 独无赵姓 - Means 'there is no surname Zhao.' '独无' means 'only without.'
  • 当他处觅耗 - Means 'to look for them in other places.' '觅耗' means 'to look for them.'
  • 榛终以神语为异 - Means 'Zhen finally found the divine words strange.' '终' means 'finally,' '以' means 'to find,' and '神语' means 'divine words.'
  • 迟徊不能去 - Means 'hesitated and could not leave.' '迟徊' means 'hesitated,' and '不能去' means 'could not leave.'
  • 忽一日，见一翁 - Means 'one day, he saw an old man.' '忽' means 'suddenly,' and '一日' means 'one day.'
  • 年七旬内，白须，行甚驶 - Describes the old man as being in his seventies, with white beard, and walking very fast. '年七旬内' means 'in his seventies,' '白须' means 'white beard,' and '行甚驶' means 'walking very fast.'
  • 辄投数镪而去 - Means 'he would immediately throw several coins and leave.' '辄' means 'immediately,' '投' means 'to throw,' '数镪' means 'several coins,' and '而去' means 'and leave.'
  • 榛甫欲问，而翁已远 - Means 'Zhen was just about to ask, but the old man had already gone far away.' '甫' means 'just,' '欲问' means 'to ask,' '而' means 'but,' and '翁已远' means 'the old man had already gone far away.'
  • 三里许，至一篱落柴门，翁即入 - Means 'about three miles away, he reached a fence and a thatched door, and the old man immediately entered.' '三里许' means 'about three miles,' '篱落柴门' means 'a fence and a thatched door,' and '翁即入' means 'the old man immediately entered.'

  • 夫不死而为嫠者也 - 'A wife who is a widow although her husband is still alive'; '嫠' means 'a widow.'
  • 访寻又不得耗 - 'Searched again but found no news'; '耗' means 'news' or 'information.'
  • 岂辞世乎？抑尚在人间乎？ - 'Has he passed away? Or is he still among the living?'; '辞世' means 'to pass away' or 'to die.'
  • 我之以为姑者，即杨氏夫所自出之母 - 'The mother who raised me as her daughter-in-law is the mother of the husband of the Yang family'; '以为姑' means 'to raise as one's mother-in-law.'
  • 生我之爱之谓何？ - 'What is the meaning of having given birth to me and loving me?'; '谓何' means 'what is the meaning of.'
  • 杨门无可抚之孤，其先人将为若敖之鬼矣！ - 'The Yang family has no one to care for the orphan, and their ancestors will become ghosts of the Ifao'; '若敖之鬼' is a classical reference to the ghosts of the Ifao clan, who were abandoned and thus became vengeful spirits.
  • 顾晋瓣香，广施大众菩萨鉴照愚忧 - 'I now burn incense in homage, and I pray that the great Bodhisattvas will see and understand my humble sorrow'; '晋瓣香' means 'to offer incense in homage' and '鉴照' means 'to see and illuminate.'

  • 甘薄幸而隳身名者 - Refers to the ex-wife's willingness to endure hardship and disgrace by marrying a man of low moral character.
  • 鲁氏 - Refers to Lu, who remained faithful and endured poverty.
  • 守贞甘贫 - Means 'to remain chaste and endure poverty,' highlighting Lu's virtue and self-discipline.
  • 凌冰霜而挺松筠 - Metaphor comparing Lu's steadfastness to the resilience of pine and bamboo in the face of harsh winter conditions.
  • 张氏 - Refers to Zhang, another character in the text.
  • 单廷玑 - A person from Shuntian (modern-day Beijing) who became a beggar.
  • 转徙而丐于江南芜湖 - Describes Single Tingji's journey to become a beggar in Wuhu, Jiangnan (southern Anhui).
  • 茧缩栗起 - Vivid description of Single Tingji's physical state, '茧缩' meaning 'contracted into a ball' and '栗起' meaning 'trembling with cold.'

Proper Nouns

  • 薛 - Xue, a character in the text.
  • 鲁氏 - Lu, a character in the text.
  • 张氏 - Zhang, a character in the text.
  • 单廷玑 - Single Tingji, a character in the text.
  • 顺天 - Shuntian, a region in northern China, now part of Beijing.
  • 江南芜湖 - Wuhu, a city in southern Anhui province.

  • 鼠偷将欲暗中摸索耶 - The rat thief is about to secretly feel around in the dark.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 单不解 - Refers to the character Dan who is puzzled by the situation.
  • 服而见翁 - Indicates a formal visit, suggesting respect and formality.
  • 我姓张，关吏也 - The speaker introduces himself as Zhang, a customs official.
  • 昔奉使令解银入都 - Refers to a past mission where he was sent to deliver silver to the capital.
  • 道被窃，银不足兑 - Describes the theft of silver and the resulting shortage.
  • 觅死 - Reflects a traditional Chinese expression of extreme distress or despair.

  • 访汝父 - Means 'visited your father.'
  • 时汝已二岁，我女亦二岁 - Indicates the age of Dan and Zhang's daughter at the time.
  • 遂与订婚姻 - Means 'we then arranged the marriage.'
  • 后四年，又进京 - Indicates another visit to the capital.
  • 汝父死 - Refers to the death of Dan's father.
  • 遍问汝 - Means 'asked everyone about you.'
  • 佥曰 - Means 'everyone said.'
  • 单贾非土著，比死则散，无可访问 - Describes the transient nature of Dan's family and the difficulty in finding them.

  • 为汝守贞至今 - Emphasizes the fidelity of Zhang's daughter.
  • 宁知汝流离若此也 - Expresses surprise and concern for Dan's current state.

  • 翁 - The old man, Zhang.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 笑置之 - Means 'laughed it off' or 'dismissed it with a laugh', indicating the father's nonchalant response.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 翁 - Refers to the father or patriarch, a common term in classical Chinese family contexts.

  • 赘单 - Refers to the practice of a man becoming a son-in-law and joining his wife's family, a common arrangement in traditional Chinese society.

  • 且言其币重，当厚赏之 - And said that his gifts were valuable, so he should be generously rewarded.
  • 督即命单代张之关吏 - The supervisor then ordered Dan to take Zhang's place as a customs officer.

  • 关钞机宜，悉为指示，不留余蕴 - He thoroughly explained the customs procedures and mechanisms, leaving nothing unsaid.
  • 为吏三年，复为鹾，积万金 - After serving as an official for three years, he returned to salt trading and accumulated ten thousand gold.
  • 遂报捐通判 - He then offered to donate money to be appointed as a magistrate.
  • 值南河清发人员，单得拣河工 - When there was a recruitment for personnel to clear the Southern River, Dan was selected to work on the river project.
  • 未逾年，为淮安府山安通判 - Within a year, he was appointed as the magistrate of Shan'an in Huai'an Prefecture.
  • 张以女年余四十，恐不育，又以次女女焉 - Zhang, fearing that his daughter, who was over forty, might not bear children, gave his second daughter in marriage to Dan.

  • 计为倅时，去丐之日才六年耳 - Calculating from when he became an assistant magistrate, it had only been six years since he started dealing with beggars.

  • 闻张翁每岁置锦衣裤施丐者，至今不倦云 - It is said that Master Zhang, every year, prepared fine clothes and trousers to give to beggars, and he has continued this practice without tiring to this day.
  • 单廷玑固无足道，独其父遇素不相识之人，慨助多金，以济急难 - Dan Tang Ji himself is not worth mentioning, but his father, who had never met the person before, generously helped with a large sum of money to relieve an urgent difficulty.

  • 张翁不以丐婿为辱，收恤而教之，亦不谓负德矣 - Master Zhang did not consider the beggar son-in-law a disgrace, but instead took care of and educated him, and he did not think he was ungrateful.
  • 至其女，以一言之约，数十年乃贞不字，誓心守义，岂不贤哉 - As for his daughter, she kept her promise of a single word for decades, remaining chaste and unmarried, vowing to uphold her virtue—how virtuous she was!
  • 卒之守义者得适所天，好义者终取其报 - In the end, the one who upheld her virtue was able to find her true match, and the one who showed kindness ultimately received her reward.
  • 而张翁且好施不倦，其事均可以劝善 - And Master Zhang, who continues to give generously without tiring, all of these things can serve as a model for good deeds.

  • 张以女年余四十，恐不育，又以次女女焉 - Zhang's concern about his daughter's fertility and his decision to give his second daughter in marriage, reflecting traditional family values.
  • 单尝与同官说丐之事，甚悉 - Dan's familiarity with the affairs of beggars, indicating his knowledge and involvement in social issues.
  • 计为倅时，去丐之日才六年耳 - The timeline of Dan's involvement with beggars, showing his long-term commitment.
  • 后迁襄河同知 - Dan's promotion to a higher position, reflecting his continued success.

  • 闻张翁每岁置锦衣裤施丐者，至今不倦云 - Master Zhang's ongoing charitable activities, showing his generosity and commitment to helping the poor.
  • 单廷玑固无足道，独其父遇素不相识之人，慨助多金，以济急难 - The father's act of kindness, highlighting the importance of generosity and helping others in need.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 性剛猛 - Stout in character or fierce in disposition, indicating a strong and unyielding personality.
  • 與人談古今不平事，辄呈愤 - Compound phrase meaning 'when talking with others about unjust matters from ancient and modern times, he would show anger.'
  • 终日不怿 - 'Remained unhappy all day,' where '怿' is a classical term for happiness or contentment.
  • 看《精忠传》，凡集中秦桧字样，皆手抠之 - 'When he read The Biography of Loyalty and Devotion, he would scratch out every occurrence of the name Qin Hui with his hands.'
  • 讼于官鞫 - 'Filed a lawsuit with the officials who conducted an investigation.'
  • 实深恶当日之桧 - 'Actually deeply hated the Qin Hui of that time.'
  • 见其似者而扑之耳 - 'He saw someone resembling him and attacked him.'
  • 官释之 - 'The officials released him.'
  • 由是乡人推服 - 'From this, the villagers came to respect and admire him.'
  • 排难解纷 - Classical expression meaning 'to resolve disputes and remove difficulties.'
  • 不避嫌怨 - 'Did not avoid suspicion or resentment.'
  • 角者即散 - 'Those who were quarreling would disperse immediately.'
  • 悻悻，寻殴不已 - 'He was furious and kept beating them.'
  • 邪无行 - 'Of evil character and no moral conduct.'
  • 披襟而歌淫亵之词 - 'Unbuckled his robe and sang indecent or lewd words.'
  • 常促邱三，唾其面目 - 'Cheng rushed at Qiu San and spat in his face.'
  • 人尚恤孤矜寡，汝凌之耶？ - 'People still care for the orphaned and widowed; do you bully them?'
  • 请试老拳 - 'Let me try my old fists' – a colloquial expression for fighting.
  • 鼻遭之而梁折，目遇之而珠流 - 'His nose was struck and the beam broke; his eyes were struck and tears flowed.' A metaphorical or hyperbolic way of describing the force of the blows.
  • 平复后，约党群殴 - 'After recovering, he gathered a group to attack him.'
  • 颅破而臂伤 - 'His head was broken and his arm was injured.'
  • 舁返 - 'Carried back,' where '舁' means to carry on a stretcher or palanquin.
  • 琴不对驴鼓，力不与牛斗 - Metaphorical saying: 'A zither should not be played against a donkey's drum; strength should not be tested against an ox.' It means one should not fight with someone much stronger.
  • 挞我于市，是可忍孰不可忍？ - 'To beat me in the marketplace—this is tolerable, what is not tolerable?' A variation of the famous Confucian saying.
  • 日伺邱 - 'Waited for Qiu every day.'
  • 色厉 - 'Appearance of anger or sternness.'
  • 肉袒 - 'To be naked or to surrender without armor,' a gesture of submission.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《精忠传》 - 'The Biography of Loyalty and Devotion,' likely a historical novel or biography about Yue Fei, the Song dynasty general who opposed the traitor Qin Hui.
  • 秦桧 - Qin Hui, a historical figure in the Southern Song dynasty, infamous for his betrayal and role in the execution of Yue Fei.
  • 武穆王 - A title for Yue Fei, known as 'Wumu Wang' (the Martial Martial King), a revered general in Chinese history.
  • 《如是观》 - A play title, possibly a classical or traditional drama.
  • 琴不对驴鼓 - A classical metaphor from Chinese literature, often used to illustrate the futility of a mismatched or unequal contest.
  • 挞我于市 - A reference to the Confucian idea of public shame and the moral implications of being beaten in public, similar to the saying 'If they can beat me in the market, what cannot they do?' (出自《论语》).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 秃梁者，乞人也 - Standard Classical Chinese structure meaning '秃梁 was a beggar.' The phrase '者...也' is a common formula for defining or identifying a subject.
  • 自幼独身行乞 - Means 'from a young age, he wandered alone begging.' '独身' here may mean 'alone' rather than 'single' in the marital sense.
  • 其顶无发 - Literally 'the top of his head had no hair,' referring to being bald.
  • 魁梧，有膂力 - 'Tall and strong, with great strength.' '膂力' is an archaic term for physical strength.
  • 有钱则买食，余以分人 - 'When he had money, he bought food; the remainder he gave to others.' The structure is elliptical but clear.
  • 或佣工，工资不计多寡 - 'Sometimes he worked as a laborer, and wages were not counted in quantity or amount.' '不计多寡' is a formal way of saying 'regardless of amount.'
  • 遇人呼修桥梁道路，不索直 - 'When people asked him to repair bridges or roads, he did not ask for payment.' '索直' is a rare phrase meaning 'to ask for payment.'
  • 有年大饥 - 'In a year of great famine.' '大饥' is a classical term for severe famine.
  • 梁乞至夷潍 - '秃梁 begged his way to Yiwéi.' '夷潍' is likely a place name, possibly a misspelling or variant of '夷潍' (Yiwéi), though its exact location is uncertain.
  • 忽大恸，诘之曰：“我思家。” - 'Suddenly he wept bitterly, and when asked, he said, 'I miss home.' '诘之' is a formal term for 'to question' or 'ask.'
  • 遽归 - 'He immediately returned home.' '遽' means 'suddenly' or 'hurriedly.'
  • 春，人相食，弃婴儿满道 - 'In spring, people ate each other, and babies were abandoned in the streets.' '相食' is a grim idiom for cannibalism.
  • 梁以两筐贮十数人，担之乞食食之 - '秃梁 used two baskets to carry ten or so people, carrying them to beg for food and feed them.' The structure is complex, with repeated use of '食' (to eat).
  • 有死者，旋补之 - 'When someone died, he immediately replaced them.' '旋补' means 'immediately replenish.'
  • 五阅月，无怠容 - 'For five months, without showing any sign of weariness.' '阅月' means 'a month' or 'a period of months.'
  • 生平不饮、不博、不盗、不与人斗 - 'Throughout his life, he did not drink, did not gamble, did not steal, and did not fight with others.' A list of vices he avoided.
  • 人托之馈遗，虽重资，一无所苟 - 'People entrusted him with gifts, even if they were valuable, he did not accept anything improperly.' '无所苟' is an archaic way of saying 'did not take anything improperly.'
  • 即大风雨，不爽期 - 'Even in heavy rain or wind, he did not miss the appointment.' '不爽期' is a formal expression meaning 'did not fail to keep the appointment.'
  • 有欲授以室者，笑而不答 - 'There were those who wanted to give him a wife, but he smiled and did not answer.' '授以室' is a classical way of saying 'to give a wife' or 'to marry.'
  • 雍正七年，病死于高密，年已七十矣 - 'In the seventh year of the Yongzheng reign, he died of illness in Gaomi, having reached the age of seventy.' '雍正七年' is a specific year in the Yongzheng era of the Qing Dynasty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 醮 - Traditional Chinese ritual of divorce or remarriage, often involving a ceremony and sometimes financial compensation.
  • 孝尔姑 - Refers to the Confucian virtue of filial piety, specifically the duty to care for one's mother-in-law.
  • 聘 - Refers to the dowry or bride price, a significant aspect of traditional Chinese marriage customs.
  • 三子 - Refers to the three sons, highlighting the importance of family and lineage in traditional Chinese society.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence is a direct question expressing a strong commitment, which should be rendered clearly in English: '既相得，当死守，宁改适?'
  • This phrase is a compound sentence explaining the reason for remarriage, which should be broken down for clarity: '醮而得金，以养以抚.'
  • This question is a logical follow-up to the previous statement, asking what will happen when the money runs out: '金有尽，姑与子畴抚养?'
  • This is a proverbial expression that should be translated to convey its cultural significance: '不贫不醮.'
  • This rhetorical question should be rendered to maintain its persuasive effect: '醮亦终贫，何如不醮?'
  • This complex phrase should be broken down to clarify the cause and effect: '迫我以不得不醮之势.'
  • This directive should be rendered clearly to convey the urgency and responsibilities: '尔急归，孝尔姑，抚诸子.'
  • These two sentences should be linked to show the relationship between the dowry and the decision not to demand it: '聘难偿，不尔索.'
  • This sentence should be rendered to show the community's approval and support: '赍尔缺，乡里称善.'
  • This sentence should be rendered to show the positive outcome of the situation: '三子皆力食，能养母矣.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 次日 - Literally 'the next day,' a common temporal marker in Classical Chinese.
  • 公升帐传集营弁 - A formal phrase meaning 'the general ascended to the tent and summoned the camp officers.' '升帐' refers to the act of raising the tent, symbolizing the general's authority.
  • 各带器械者，听骂曰 - A phrase indicating that the officers were ordered to bring their weapons and were then subjected to verbal abuse. '听骂' means 'to be subjected to scolding.'
  • 余结发从戎，身经百战 - A self-referential statement meaning 'I have been a soldier since my youth, having fought in countless battles.' '结发' refers to the beginning of one's adult life, often associated with marriage, but here it metaphorically signifies the start of a soldier's career.
  • 汝曹竖子，何敢当万人敌 - A rhetorical question meaning 'How dare you, mere boys, face the enemy of ten thousand?' '汝曹' is a collective pronoun meaning 'you all,' and '竖子' is a derogatory term for 'young men' or 'fools.'
  • 遂试武于庭，距跃过辕门 - A phrase describing a demonstration of martial prowess, meaning 'he then demonstrated his martial skills in the courtyard, leaping over the gate.' '距跃' means 'leaping with one foot forward.'
  • 复袒衣，创痕瘢癜 - A description of the general revealing his injuries, meaning 'he then bared his chest, showing the scars of his wounds.' '创痕瘢癜' refers to the marks of past injuries.
  • 众皆啮齿咋舌，罗拜于地 - A vivid description of the officers' reaction, meaning 'everyone bit their lips and gasped, prostrating themselves on the ground.' '啮齿咋舌' is an expression of awe or fear.
  • 乃追其余党，各重捶之 - A phrase indicating the general's punishment of his subordinates, meaning 'he then pursued the rest of his followers, beating each of them severely.' '重捶' means 'severe beating.'
  • 自是营伍辑服，奉张公约 - A conclusion indicating the aftermath, meaning 'from then on, the camp was orderly and disciplined, following the general's orders.' '辑服' means 'orderly and disciplined.'

If anyone tried to chase him, Zhou would draw his knife and fight bravely, making him invincible. The nickname 'Pida' originated from this. Yesterday, he was drunk and snoring loudly, sleeping in a wild temple, so they were able to capture him. Otherwise, he would not have been easy to capture.
  • 赵乃指周曰：“囚，何说之辞？” - Zhao then pointed to Zhou and asked, 'Prisoner, what do you have to say?'

Now unfortunately, I am in chains, I am not like a petty thief begging for mercy. I admit I am a thief, I believe you would not punish me for killing cattle and horses as if I had committed murder.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 狐能窃之，即可以为仙 - If a fox can steal these, it can become a deity. This is a metaphorical statement about the transformative power of knowledge.
  • 极之，窃宝玉，窃大弓，窃虎符 - Extremely, stealing precious jade, a great bow, or a tiger talisman. This is a list of valuable items that can be stolen.

  • 又何窃之义类之足充与 - How can such acts fill the category of theft? This is a rhetorical question challenging the classification of minor theft.
  • 窃狁囚乎哉 - Is theft a prisoner? This is a rhetorical question questioning the nature of theft.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 婦早起，捧雕盤，盛熊燔一{目廷}，炙馨欲染指 - Describes a woman preparing a dish early in the morning, with a beautifully carved plate and roasted bear meat that smells so good it makes one want to lick their fingers.
  • 使驰馈 - Means sending someone to deliver the food.
  • 受而甘之，报谢 - Indicates receiving the food with pleasure and expressing gratitude.
  • 及公谒抚，抚曰：“承贶嘉珍，安得此善庖丁？我府中刀俎不及也。” - Describes a public official (抚) receiving the food and complimenting the cook, asking how such a skilled chef could be found.
  • 藩曰：“适来浣妇，初不知其工调剂。宪军如适口，当使其越俎而代。” - The official (藩) explains that the woman who washed clothes was unexpectedly skilled in cooking.
  • 抚乐甚，留不返 - The official is very pleased and decides to stay.
  • 且邀藩饮，一切酸咸，皆出妇手，不假咄嗟 - The official invites the other official (藩) to drink, and all the dishes are prepared by the woman, without any delay.
  • 抚每往狎妇，妇固黠甚，抚不可耐，要于槛而约之 - The official frequently visits the woman, who is very clever, and the official becomes impatient, making a promise to her.
  • 妇曰：“大人高贵，贱妾躯龌龊，不足荐枕席。”抚坚之，妇乃约曰：“室南绮轩，薄暮请俟妾于轩中。” - The woman modestly declines, but the official insists, and she agrees to meet him in a pavilion in the south of the room in the evening.
  • 抚候之晚。时当秋凉，日甫昵，抚纱夹摇羽箑，大椅坐夜番棚下 - The official waits for her in the evening, and it is a cool autumn day, with the sun setting, and he sits on a large chair in a bamboo hut, fanning himself with a feather fan.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 熊燔一{目廷} - Roasted bear meat, a delicacy in ancient China.

  • 绮轩 - A beautifully decorated pavilion or small building.
  • 夜番棚 - A bamboo hut used for shelter, often associated with rural or simple living.

  • This sequence describes the official’s pleasure and decision to stay.
  • This sequence describes the official’s invitation to drink and the woman’s skill in preparing all the dishes.
  • This sequence describes the official’s frequent visits and the woman’s cleverness.
  • This sequence describes the woman’s modesty and the official’s insistence, leading to a promise to meet in a pavilion.
  • This sequence describes the official’s wait and the setting in which he waits.

I dwell at the top of Qu Mountain, where the fresh breeze blows day and night, reflecting your form and illuminating your heart. No matter how far, I can reach you with a breath.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 抚之发髯须眉衣裳，层层剥削，满地如尘 - Describes a process of stripping away layers, like peeling an egg or scraping a gourd, and leaving behind a mess. '层层剥削' (layer by layer stripping) conveys the thoroughness of the action.
  • 如剥卵，如刮瓠，三月不视谒 - Similes comparing the action of stripping to peeling an egg and scraping a gourd, emphasizing the meticulous and perhaps destructive nature of the process. The subsequent isolation for three months is a result of this action.

  • 客京师之粉坊胡同南口 - Indicates that he resides in a specific location in the capital city, which was likely a bustling area during the time.
  • 邻多隙地，近苇塘 - Describes the setting near his residence, with nearby vacant land and reed ponds, suggesting a somewhat isolated or rural environment within the city.
  • 与某藩前怨亦释 - Refers to the resolution of a previous conflict or grudge with a certain noble, though the specific identity is not given.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 婦不苟於言笑 - Literally 'the wife is not frivolous in speech and laughter,' meaning she is serious and reserved.
  • 事齊颇勤 - Literally 'serving Qi very diligently,' indicating her hard work and dedication.
  • 京城米珠薪桂 - A proverb meaning 'rice is as expensive as pearls, firewood as expensive as cinnamon,' indicating the high cost of living in the capital.
  • 居大不易 - Literally 'it is not easy to live in the capital,' a common expression indicating the difficulty of making a living in a large city.
  • 食指又增 - Literally 'the number of people to be fed has increased,' meaning the family has grown or there are more mouths to feed.
  • 當思所以治生者 - Literally 'one should think of ways to make a living,' suggesting the need to find a means of income.
  • 磨得面，辄用驴驮，自鬻于市 - Literally 'grind flour, then use donkeys to carry it, and sell it in the market,' describing a practical method of earning money.
  • 至晚归，则麦囊中垂垂皆钱也 - Literally 'by evening, when she returns, the wheat bag is full of money,' indicating successful sales.
  • 齐入课成均 - Literally 'Qi enters the Imperial Academy,' meaning Qi is studying at a prestigious institution.
  • 多不家 - Literally 'he does not stay at home much,' indicating Qi spends a lot of time away from home.
  • 又复得膏金 - Literally 'he also receives a large sum of money,' suggesting additional financial gains.
  • 妇善生理 - Literally 'the wife is good at managing the household,' indicating her skill in household management.
  • 齐之客旅将丰于其家 - Literally 'Qi's travels will make the family prosperous,' suggesting that Qi's activities will bring wealth to the family.
  • 从无柴米拮据 - Literally 'there is never a shortage of firewood and rice,' indicating that the family never lacks basic necessities.
  • 墙外有腰斩一尸，无上段 - Literally 'outside the wall, there is a decapitated body with no head,' describing a violent crime.
  • 京师汹汹然 - Literally 'the capital is in a state of panic,' indicating widespread fear and unrest.
  • 客坊初不以为齐妇，即齐亦断不以为妇之为之也 - Literally 'the innkeepers initially did not suspect Qi's wife, and Qi also did not think it was his wife who did it,' indicating the initial disbelief and denial.
  • 逾年未缉获，事宕 - Literally 'for over a year, the case remained unsolved,' indicating the prolonged investigation.
  • 后妇产一子，齐肄业将满 - Literally 'later, the wife gave birth to a son, and Qi was about to complete his studies,' indicating the progression of time and events.
  • 每言欲与妇同归江南，妇但微哂，亦不答 - Literally 'every time he mentioned wanting to return to Jiangnan with his wife, she only smiled slightly and did not respond,' indicating her subtle reaction.
  • 时夜半，齐寝，闭户垂帏，忽失妇所在 - Literally 'one night, Qi slept, closed the door, and hung the curtain, but suddenly found his wife missing,' indicating a sudden and mysterious disappearance.
  • 以为有奸，颇发怒愤 - Literally 'he thought she was having an affair and became very angry,' indicating his suspicion and anger.
  • 问其婢，曰：“娘子每常如是，不知所为。郎君特不知觉耳。” - Literally 'he asked his servant, who said, 'Madam often does this, I don't know why. You just don't notice it.'', indicating the servant's explanation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 数年以来，此仇已报，克不可留 - Means 'For several years, this grudge has been avenged, and it cannot be left behind,' indicating the woman's determination to complete her mission.

  • 女奴是妾数年所抚，郎可纳之，以代我任，且育汝子 - Means 'The maid has been raised by me for many years; you may take her as a concubine to replace me and raise your child,' showing the woman's concern for Qi's future.
  • 言讫收泪，逾重垣，莫知其向 - Means 'After finishing her words, she wiped away her tears, leaped over a high wall, and no one knew where she went,' indicating the woman's departure.
  • 入室不闻儿啼，视之儿已身首异处矣 - Means 'Upon entering the room, he did not hear the child crying; upon looking, he saw that the child had been beheaded,' revealing the tragic fate of Qi's child.
  • 女奴曰：“妾十岁，父母鬻于娘子。娘子育之，五年而不知娘子为谁也。” - Means 'The maid said, 'I am ten years old, my parents sold me to you. You raised me, and for five years, I did not know who you were,' providing background information about the maid.
  • 齐令女密其事，纳之为妾 - Means 'Qi ordered the maid to keep the matter secret and took her as a concubine,' showing Qi's decision to take the maid into his household.
  • 是年，齐得官，为东川云阳丞 - Means 'That year, Qi obtained an official position as the magistrate of Yunyang in Dongchuan,' indicating Qi's rise in social status.
  • 后终不闻妇之音问也 - Means 'Later, he never heard from the woman again,' indicating the end of the woman's involvement in Qi's life.
  • 传奇中《锁云囊》有女盗挂须髯，绝相类 - Means 'In the novel *The Locked Cloud Sack*, there is a female thief who wears a beard, which is extremely similar,' providing a cultural reference to a similar story.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 會徵宏博，擢第二 - When he was selected for the grand examination, he was ranked second. '宏博' refers to a high-level imperial examination, and '擢第二' means being ranked second in the examination.
  • 晚歲至滇黔節制 - In his later years, he reached a position of military control in Yunnan and Guizhou. '節制' refers to a military governor or commander.
  • 咸以為忠厚之報 - Everyone believed it was a reward for his loyalty and kindness. '忠厚之報' suggests that his success was seen as a result of his moral virtues.
  • 冬烘一生行谊 - A life of conduct as bland and unremarkable as winter fog. '冬烘' is a metaphor for dullness or lack of brilliance.
  • 老树着花 - Flowers blooming on an old tree. A metaphor for unexpected beauty or success in old age.
  • 无一丑枝而古艳 - Not a single ugly branch, yet anciently beautiful. This emphasizes the elegance and grace of his character.
  • 跃跃纸上 - Leaping out of the paper. A vivid metaphor for something vividly portrayed or lifelike.
  • 悃款出于自然 - Sincerity and honesty coming naturally. '悃款' refers to genuine and sincere behavior.
  • 风流亦自不免 - He could not escape being elegant and refined. '风流' can mean elegance, refinement, or even a certain charm.
  • 时对此篇犹令人神往于函丈春容际耳 - At that time, reading this piece still makes one yearn for the springtime atmosphere of the master's presence. '函丈' refers to the distance between a teacher and student, and '春容' suggests a spring-like atmosphere of warmth and vitality.
  • 豫章省城外，有黄牛洲 - Outside the provincial capital of Yuzhang, there is a place called Huangniuzhou. '豫章' is the ancient name for Nanchang, the capital of Jiangxi province.
  • 尝商于闽、广间，航海上下，数十年也 - He once traded in Fujian and Guangdong, sailing up and down the seas for many years. '航海上下' indicates frequent maritime travel.
  • 乌语数间，乐意可聆 - The calls of the birds in a few trees can be pleasant to listen to. '乐意' means pleasant or enjoyable.
  • 今人笼之棘中，以听其呼朋哀怨之声，亦复何也 - Now people cage them in thorns to hear their cries of longing and sorrow, what for? This criticizes the cruelty of trapping birds.
  • 自揣万无生理 - He thought to himself that there was no chance of survival. '自揣' means to think or reflect to oneself.
  • 忽觉身畔有木 - Suddenly, he felt something wooden beside him. '身畔' means beside or near oneself.
  • 江抱之，木起江起，浪落身落 - Jiang clung to it, and as the wood rose, so did he; as the waves fell, so did he. This describes a miraculous rescue.
  • 浮沉出没一日夜 - He floated and sank for a whole day and night. '浮沉出没' describes being tossed by the waves.
  • 江力尽，风愈狂 - Jiang was exhausted, and the wind became even more fierce. '力尽' means to be completely drained of strength.
  • 随波至岸，觉水浅，身不自持，海浪推沙于身际，犹相击也 - He was carried by the waves to the shore, feeling the water was shallow, unable to support himself, with the sea waves still pushing sand against him. This describes the aftermath of the rescue.

  • 老树着花 - A metaphor for unexpected success or beauty in old age.
  • 悃款 - Refers to genuine and sincere behavior.
  • 风流 - Can mean elegance, refinement, or charm, often used to describe a person's demeanor.
  • 函丈 - The distance between a teacher and student, symbolizing the relationship between a master and disciple.
  • 春容 - A spring-like atmosphere, often used to describe a warm and lively environment.

  • 闽、广 - Refers to Fujian and Guangdong provinces, known for their maritime trade.
  • 航海上下 - Frequent maritime travel, indicating a life of seafaring.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一日 - A common temporal marker meaning 'one day' or 'on a day.'
  • 侦知其饮于姻家 - '侦知' means to discover or learn through investigation; '姻家' is a term for a relative by marriage, often used to refer to a spouse's family. The phrase means 'discovered that he was drinking at his in-laws' house.'
  • 更余 - A time marker meaning 'after the first watch' or 'well past the first hour of the night' (in the traditional Chinese night-watch system).
  • 贫，我命也 - 'Poorness is my fate' or 'Poverty is my destiny.' A common expression of resignation to one's circumstances.
  • 某谋产而得产 - 'I schemed to obtain property and succeeded.' This is a self-referential statement about the narrator’s actions.
  • 渠自昧心 - 'He [the victim] has his own conscience obscured' or 'He [the victim] has lost his sense of right and wrong.'
  • 我复舍命而杀人 - 'I again risk my life to kill.' Indicates the speaker's willingness to commit murder.
  • 我仍无产且亦丧命，何益之有？ - 'I would still have no property and would lose my life—what benefit is there in that?' A rhetorical question expressing futility.
  • 遂掷刀于河 - 'He then threw the knife into the river.' A decisive action.
  • 返走暗中 - 'He turned and ran into the shadows.' Indicates retreat.
  • 度石桥 - 'Crossed a stone bridge.' A literal description of movement.
  • 觉有人自驼后扳倒仆地 - 'Felt someone from behind pull him down and knock him to the ground.' Indicates an attack from behind.
  • 又似一人持二板至 - 'Then someone else seemed to come with two boards.' Describes the arrival of a second figure.
  • 遂置驼于板上，复以一板压之，缚自勒板，如榨油麻 - 'Then placed the camel on the board, and placed another board on top, binding him and pressing down, like pressing oil from hemp.' This is a metaphor for a squeezing or pressing action, likely a torture device or mechanical press.
  • 驼本枉者，而使之直 - 'The camel, originally crooked, is made straight.' A metaphor for transformation or correction.
  • 是犹以桮棬为杞柳也 - 'It is like using cups and bowls to make a willow tree.' A reference to a classical metaphor from *Mencius* about transformation through shaping. *桮棬* (bowls and cups) are made from *杞柳* (willow), implying that something crooked can be made straight through shaping.
  • 驼觉腰背悉为夹碎，痛急昏去 - 'The camel felt his back and waist crushed between the boards, the pain so intense that he fainted.' A vivid description of physical suffering.
  • 复苏，一无所有 - 'Revived, he found nothing.' Indicates loss of the object or transformation.
  • 反手腰背，大异于前 - 'When he turned his hands to his back, it was greatly different from before.' Describes the physical transformation.
  • 疾返叩门 - 'He hurried back and knocked on the door.' Indicates urgency.
  • 惊笑达比邻，共走视，果无复拳曲故态 - 'The surprise and laughter reached the neighbors, who all ran to see, and indeed, he had no more of his old bent form.' Describes the reaction of the community.
  • 远近传为异事 - 'Far and near, it was spread as a strange event.' Indicates the story’s notoriety.
  • 稍有周给之者，驼又小康 - 'Those who slightly helped him, the camel became moderately prosperous again.' Suggests that the transformation brought some material benefit.
  • 人问之，诡言得一秘方，而挟刀事密不言 - 'When people asked him, he lied and said he had found a secret formula, and kept the matter of the knife a secret.' Indicates the narrator's attempt to conceal the truth.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 姻家 - A term for a relative by marriage, often used to refer to the family of one's spouse. This is a common term in classical Chinese, indicating social relationships.
  • 更余 - Refers to the traditional Chinese timekeeping system, where the night was divided into five 'watches' (更), each lasting about two hours. '更余' means 'well past the first watch.'
  • 金元宝 - A 'golden ingot' or 'gold bar,' a common form of currency in ancient China. Its mention may imply that the camel was involved in a theft or a financial scheme.
  • 榨油麻 - A reference to the process of extracting oil from hemp, which was a common agricultural practice. The metaphor here is used to describe the intense pressure applied to the camel.
  • 桮棬为杞柳 - A reference to a well-known metaphor from *Mencius* (孟子), where he says that a willow can be made into a drinking vessel (桮棬), implying that even something inherently crooked can be shaped into something useful. This metaphor is used here to suggest that the camel, once deformed, is now straightened and transformed.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夫幕猶医也 - Classical opening formula meaning 'A *mù* is like a physician.' *夫* is a discourse marker, *猶* means 'is like' or 'is similar to.'
  • 良相之无异于良医者 - 'A good chancellor is no different from a good physician.' *无异于* means 'is no different from.'
  • 不以其事之悬绝，而力之足以活人，一也 - 'Not because their tasks are vastly different, but because their ability to save lives is the same, that is one reason.' *悬绝* means 'vastly different' or 'far apart'; *一也* is a concluding phrase meaning 'that is one reason' or 'this is the first point.'
  • 士之不得志于时，借术托途 - 'Scholars who are not favored by the times use their skills as a means of survival.' *不得志于时* means 'not favored by the times' or 'not achieving success in their time'; *借术托途* means 'to use skill as a way to survive' or 'to rely on a method to find a path.'
  • 岂但糊口，最好积善 - 'It is not merely to make a living, but best to accumulate virtue.' *岂但* is a rhetorical question meaning 'not only' or 'not just'; *最好积善* means 'it is best to accumulate virtue.'
  • 肯堂分文不费，可以救人之危 - 'If one is willing to spend not a single coin, one can save people from danger.' *分文不费* is an idiom meaning 'not a single coin' or 'not even a penny.'
  • 构堂声色不动，可以全人之命 - 'If one remains unmoved by pleasure or distraction, one can preserve a person's life.' *声色不动* is an idiom meaning 'unmoved by pleasure or sensory indulgence'; *全人之命* means 'preserve a person's life.'
  • 顾何惮而不为，乃刻薄若是？ - 'Why then should one hesitate to do it, and yet be so stingy?' *顾何惮而不为* is a rhetorical question meaning 'why hesitate not to do it?' *刻薄若是* means 'so stingy' or 'so mean-spirited.'
  • 无他，见利而忘义也 - 'There is no other reason, but that they see profit and forget righteousness.' *无他* means 'no other reason'; *见利而忘义* is a classical expression meaning 'to see profit and forget righteousness.'
  • 虽称名幕，而搁笔辄穷老而潦倒 - 'Although called a famous *mù*, once the pen is put down, they become poor and destitute in old age.' *搁笔辄穷老而潦倒* is a compound expression meaning 'once they stop writing, they become poor and destitute in old age.'
  • 七如氏曰 - A classical way of introducing a commentary or afterword, similar to 'The commentator says' or 'The author remarks.'

  • 特遣將校赍重幣往求 - Means 'especially sent officers with heavy gifts to seek.'
  • 陳遲欠未予 - Means 'Chen delayed and did not give.'
  • 登堂坐索 - Means 'entered the hall and sat to demand.'
  • 頗事羅唣 - Means 'caused a lot of disturbance' or 'made a fuss.'
  • 斥逐之 - Means 'scolded and drove them away.'
  • 遷怒於毛 - Means 'transferred anger to Mao.'
  • 黨將軍帳下羔酒習氣 - Refers to the 'sheep and wine habits' of the general's entourage, possibly a metaphor for indulgence or excess.
  • 何足掛懷 - Means 'not worth worrying about.'
  • 門人某 - Refers to a disciple or student of someone.
  • 兵部尚書 - A high-ranking official in the Ministry of War.
  • 過訪求教 - Means 'visited to seek instruction.'
  • 拔一毛可以利天下 - A reference to the saying '拔一毛而利天下，不為也,' meaning 'to pluck a single hair for the benefit of the world, I would not do it,' often attributed to Yang Zhu.
  • 再拜謝曰 - Means 'bowed twice and thanked.'
  • 履任 - Means 'took office' or 'assumed the position.'
  • 以罪狀而诛之 - Means 'imputed crimes and executed him.'
  • 邊事大坏 - Means 'the border situation deteriorated greatly.'
  • 論者以明三百年天下，实眉公一言亡之也 - Means 'those who commented on it said that the Ming dynasty's three hundred years of rule was actually lost due to one word from Mei Gong.'
  • 殿元公 - Refers to a high-ranking official, possibly a状元 (zhuangyuan), the top imperial examination candidate.
  • 遭雷殛死 - Means 'was struck by lightning and died.'
  • 成殓后，雷复震其尸 - Means 'after the funeral, lightning struck his body again.'
  • 荆宜观察 - Refers to a position of an observer or inspector in Jing and Yi provinces.
  • 准人筑州种苇 - Means 'allowed people to build a state and plant reeds.'
  • 堵截江流 - Means 'blocked the river flow.'
  • 贻灌城决堤之患 - Means 'caused flooding and dike breaches.'
  • 数世诛锄 - Means 'punishment across generations.'
  • 白起牛 - Refers to Bai Qi, a famous general, and '牛' (cow) might be a metaphor for his cruelty or power.
  • 曹瞒豕 - Refers to Cao Cao, a historical figure, and '豕' (pig) might be a metaphor for his cunning or ruthlessness.

  • 火盛水沸 - Means 'the fire was intense and the water was boiling.'
  • 鳖头出口张 - Means 'the turtle's head emerged and opened.'
  • 醢酱姜桂之属 - Refers to various seasonings like fermented meat, soy sauce, ginger, and cinnamon.
  • 杓而饮之 - Means 'poured and drank it.'
  • 鳖熟而味已入矣 - Means 'the turtle was cooked, and the flavors had already been absorbed.'
  • 如是有年 - Means 'like this for many years.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一夕 - Classical expression meaning 'one night', setting the temporal context.
  • 火发 - Literally 'fire breaks out', a concise way of describing a fire starting.
  • 僧故楼居 - 'The monk lived in a tower', with '故' indicating habitual residence.
  • 思钻月窗以遁 - 'Thought of drilling through the moonlit window to escape', a vivid and somewhat unusual escape plan.
  • 窗小，仅容一首 - 'The window was small, only large enough for one head', emphasizing the narrowness of the window.
  • 竟烧死 - 'Ultimately burned to death', indicating the tragic outcome.
  • 观者曰 - 'The observers said', introducing a commentary.
  • 今日之烧死僧，如当日之活煮鳖 - 'The monk who burned to death today is like the turtle that was alive and then boiled', a metaphorical comparison.
  • 甲鱼同苋菜食，生鳖，茅舍潺滴肉上，皆可杀人 - 'If a turtle is eaten with water spinach, if it's a live turtle, or if it's placed on meat in a thatched cottage where water drips, all can kill', a reference to traditional beliefs about food combinations.
  • 往往不辨，食之主血胀死 - 'Often indistinguishable, eating it causes blood to swell and die', another reference to traditional beliefs about food safety.
  • 济宁三井闸 - 'The Three Wells Lock in Jinan', a specific location.
  • 为运河蓄泄湖水而筑 - 'Built to store and release lake water for the canal', explaining the purpose of the lock.
  • 粮艘至，起板迎溜以上 - 'When grain ships arrive, boards are raised to meet the current and move upstream', describing the process of navigating the lock.
  • 千夫牵挽，声振断流 - 'Thousands of people pull and tug, the sound shaking the broken flow', a vivid description of the effort required.
  • 饿鬼道中，往往托生于此 - 'Often reborn here in the realm of hungry ghosts', a Buddhist concept.
  • 友人有悯粮艘纤夫、集唐一首云 - 'A friend, moved by the plight of the grain ship纤夫, compiled a poem from Tang verses', introducing a poem.
  • 西江运船立红帜 - 'Ships on the West River stand with red flags', a vivid image of the scene.
  • 落帆渡桥来浦里 - 'Lowering sails to cross the bridge and enter the harbor', describing the arrival of the ships.
  • 送风上水万斛里 - 'Sending the wind to push the ships up the water, carrying ten thousand measures', a metaphor for the effort required.
  • 自怜淮海同泥滓 - 'Lamenting that the Huai and Hai rivers are like mud and silt', expressing a sense of hardship.
  • 计合一条麻绳挽 - 'Calculating that one hemp rope can pull', a reference to the strength required.
  • 有力未免遭驱使 - 'Even the strong cannot avoid being driven', a comment on the harsh conditions.
  • 邮夫防吏急喧驱 - 'Postal workers and guards rush and shout to drive them', describing the urgency and pressure.
  • 夜间鼍声人尽起 - 'At night, the crocodile sounds make everyone wake up', a vivid description of the environment.
  • 不辞手足皆胼胝 - 'Not sparing hands and feet, all calloused', a description of the physical toll.
  • 趚趚踏沙人似鬼 - 'Stepping on the sand, people look like ghosts', a vivid image of the workers.
  • 尔来气少筋骨露 - 'Since then, their strength has diminished, and their bones show', a description of the workers' condition.
  • 因风因雨更憔悴 - 'Due to wind and rain, they become even more emaciated', a description of the workers' suffering.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 茫茫漫漫方自悲 - Poetic expression meaning 'in the vast and boundless expanse, one feels sorrow,' conveying a sense of existential loneliness or despair.
  • 顽钝如船命如纸 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'stupid as a boat and life as thin as paper,' suggesting a life of hardship and fragility.
  • 柳丝挽断肠牵断 - Poetic expression meaning 'the willow threads can tear the heart apart,' symbolizing intense emotional pain.
  • 千声万血谁哀尔 - Rhetorical question meaning 'who will mourn you in your thousands of cries and bloodshed,' emphasizing the lack of compassion for the suffering.
  • 呜呼余心诚恺悌 - Exclamatory phrase meaning 'alas, my heart is truly kind and gentle,' expressing a sincere and compassionate sentiment.
  • 莫言自古皆如此 - Cautionary phrase meaning 'do not say that this has always been so,' suggesting that not all situations are inevitable or unchangeable.
  • 谁人为奏圣天子 - Rhetorical question meaning 'who will present this to the holy emperor,' implying a call for justice or reform.
  • 人为之也 - Straightforward statement meaning 'it was done by humans,' indicating that the deformity is a result of human action.
  • 种麦一年，供骰一箝；种秫一秋，打叶一周 - Colloquial expression describing the cycle of gambling and loss, where planting crops yields only enough to sustain gambling debts.
  • 家有薄产，耽于赌，故种麦一年，供骰一箝；种秫一秋，打叶一周 - Describes a lifestyle of poverty and gambling addiction, where farming is done solely to fund gambling activities.
  • 岁将暮，家家办酒果，而李冰釜冷灶，若度寒食禁烟 - Contrast between the festive preparations of others and the cold, desolate state of Li's home.
  • 酒肉，朋友也；柴米，夫妻也 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'wine and meat are friends; firewood and rice are spouses,' highlighting the importance of basic necessities in marriage.
  • 我自嫁汝家，终岁操作，不曾换得一餐饱 - Statement of hardship and neglect in marriage, where the wife has worked hard all year without ever being able to eat her fill.
  • 岁将尽，尔其与之俱尽乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'is your life coming to an end along with the year?' expressing frustration and despair.
  • 我将觅自尽 - Statement of intent to commit suicide, indicating deep despair and hopelessness.
  • 尽在树间 - Cryptic response, possibly meaning 'it's all in the tree,' which the wife interprets as a metaphor for her husband's plan.
  • 愤然取厨刀，断其树 - Describes the husband's violent and impulsive action of cutting down the tree.
  • 睨而视之，窃有所喜 - Description of the husband's reaction, where he looks at the tree and secretly feels pleased, suggesting a twisted sense of satisfaction.
  • 以为可使制梃而御人于国门之外矣 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'he thought he could use a stick to fend off people at the city gate,' indicating a delusional belief in his ability to protect himself.
  • 芟繁柯，伐碎叶，应手而去 - Description of the husband's actions in pruning and cutting the tree, emphasizing his meticulous and determined effort.
  • 妻亦不问其所之 - Statement indicating that the wife does not question her husband's actions, possibly due to her own despair and resignation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 出官道，伏柳树下 - Leaving the official road and lying in ambush under a willow tree, suggesting a hidden or secretive action.
  • 夜北风凛凛，一人负行李踉跄来 - A cold northern wind at night, with a person carrying luggage coming staggering, indicating fatigue or urgency.
  • 棒喝之，其人惧，遗所负以逸 - Striking and shouting, causing the person to be afraid and abandon what he was carrying to escape, showing fear and desperation.
  • 李喜，固利在物不在人 - Li was pleased, because he believed the gain was in the objects, not in the person, highlighting a materialistic mindset.
  • 归，启视钱物 - Returning and opening to see the money and goods, indicating a return to check the spoils.
  • 新衣，颇足办五辛盘 - New clothes, quite enough to make a five-spice dish, suggesting a significant amount of wealth or provisions.
  • 夫妻皆欣欣度乐岁 - The husband and wife both happily spent the happy year, indicating a joyful and prosperous year.
  • 第倘来物，不甚爱惜 - But the things that came by chance were not cherished very much, suggesting a lack of appreciation for unexpected gains.
  • 曾几何时，瓶罍告匮 - Not long after, the jars and bottles were empty, indicating a quick depletion of resources.
  • 李复技痒 - Li again felt the urge to act, suggesting a recurrence of his previous behavior.
  • 妇谏曰：“得意不宜再往。” - His wife advised, 'It's not right to go again when you're already satisfied,' indicating a warning against overconfidence.
  • 不听，复要于路 - He didn't listen and again set out on the road, showing disregard for advice.
  • 月朦胧上 - The moon was dimly rising, setting a mysterious and dimly lit scene.
  • 见驴背大囊，一老叟盹而骑 - He saw a large bag on a donkey, with an old man dozing and riding, describing a specific encounter.
  • 去三步，击之，不中 - He took three steps and struck, but missed, indicating an attempt that failed.
  • 叟下，撤梃，前步，提李发立起 - The old man got off, pulled out his staff, stepped forward, and pulled Li up by his hair, showing a confrontation.
  • 若是谁 - Who are you? A direct question.
  • 不答，复问，李亦不答 - He didn't answer, and when asked again, Li also didn't answer, indicating stubbornness or fear.
  • 叟以足略拨，李仆地仰 - The old man slightly kicked him, and Li fell face up, showing a physical interaction.
  • 叟踏李胸，曰：“汝不言，且试汝梃。” - The old man stepped on Li's chest and said, 'You won't talk? Let me try your stick,' indicating a threat.
  • 一梃而齿牙脱，再梃而鼻梁折，三梃而眉飞目去 - One strike and his teeth fell out, another and his nose broke, and a third and his eyebrows and eyes flew away, describing a series of violent strikes.
  • 如荠辛臼，千捶百捣，至无口无耳无鼻舌身意 - Like a radish in a mortar, beaten a thousand times, until he had no mouth, no ears, no nose, no tongue, no body, no mind, describing a state of complete disfigurement.
  • 更幻出一切不可思议诸般色相 - Then he transformed into all kinds of unimaginable forms, suggesting a supernatural transformation.
  • 叟兴尽，复跨蹇迢迢而去 - The old man, having had his fill, remounted his donkey and went away slowly, indicating the end of the confrontation.
  • 李死而复苏，血与泪迸 - Li died but revived, with blood and tears gushing out, showing a dramatic and painful recovery.
  • 我复有何面目返家门对妻子耶 - I have no face to return home to my wife and children, expressing shame and regret.
  • 遂流于今盖二十年 - And thus he has been wandering for about twenty years, indicating a long period of wandering.
  • 余异其状，故备书之 - I find his appearance strange, so I have written it all down, indicating the author's account of the story.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 岁暮 - Refers to the end of the year, often associated with the Chinese New Year and the return home for family reunions.
  • 五辛盘 - A traditional Chinese dish made with five types of vegetables, often served during the Lunar New Year.
  • 棒喝 - Originally a Zen Buddhist term meaning 'stick and shout,' used to shock or awaken someone to a higher state of consciousness. Here, it is used in a more literal sense of violence.
  • 无口无耳无鼻舌身意 - Refers to the loss of all senses and physical attributes, a metaphorical expression of complete disfigurement or transformation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 祈梦 - Seeking guidance or insight through dreams, often associated with divination.
  • 定终身、卜休咎 - Determining one's fate and predicting good or bad omens.
  • 二八月闻 - Refers to the second and eighth months of the lunar calendar.
  • 斋宿庙中 - Abstaining and staying overnight in the temple as part of a ritual preparation.
  • 于少保忠肃公庙 - A temple dedicated to Yu Shao, a historical figure known for his integrity and loyalty.
  • 于公年少时好梦 - Yu Gong, when young, had frequent dreams, suggesting a personal habit or predisposition.
  • 汝终身，归问汝嫂，则知之 - A cryptic message from the dream, instructing to ask one's sister-in-law for knowledge.
  • 夭杀的，不过与你一二品小官足矣 - A sarcastic or dismissive response, implying that a minor official position is sufficient.
  • 少保 - A high-ranking official title, indicating Yu Shao's status.
  • 英宗复辟 - The restoration of Emperor Yingzong to the throne.
  • 遂及难 - And thus he met his fate, implying a tragic end.
  • 若怨于梦 - As if blaming the dream, suggesting a sense of dissatisfaction or frustration.

  • 穷于遇 - Lacking in fortune or unlucky in life.
  • 困于学 - Struggling with studies or having difficulty in learning.
  • 幼婚于富伧张姓 - Married at a young age to a wealthy merchant family named Zhang.

  • 周次倩 - Zhou is the second son-in-law, indicating his position among Zhang's sons-in-law.
  • 鹾商子 - Son of a salt merchant, referring to the first son-in-law.
  • 张翁每爱任而鄙周 - Old Master Zhang always favored Ren and looked down on Zhou, indicating a family hierarchy.
  • 周亦遂不敢与任齿 - Zhou also dared not compare himself to Ren, showing his inferior position.
  • 会张翁寿辰 - On the occasion of Old Master Zhang's birthday.

  • 会安抚道出青阳 - 'Just then, the governor passed through Qingyang,' indicating a timely and significant event.

  • 抚以为妄，饬邑令止之 - 'The governor considered it nonsense and ordered the county magistrate to stop it,' indicating an attempt to suppress the celebration.
  • 及秋，抚军监临闱事 - 'By autumn, the governor was overseeing the examination,' indicating the timing of the imperial examination.
  • 填榜之夕，将拆弥封 - 'On the night the list was being filled, they were about to open the sealed exam papers,' referring to the official process of announcing the results.
  • 至二十一名，抚军起言于主试者曰 - 'When they reached the 21st name, the governor stood up and said to the chief examiner,' indicating a moment of intervention.
  • 下官春巡，道经青阳，见有汪姓祠堂贺新举人者，并署二十一名 - 'During my spring tour, I passed through Qingyang and saw the Wang family’s ancestral hall celebrating a new successful candidate, who was listed as 21st,' indicating a memory of the earlier event.
  • 问其故，则以梦征 - 'I asked why, and they said it was based on a dream,' indicating the origin of the celebration.
  • 今拆号至此，恐前日之托于梦者，或今日之不免于贿也 - 'Now that we have reached this number, I fear that the one who relied on a dream before may now be involved in bribery,' suggesting a suspicion of corruption.
  • 力破其关节 - 'To thoroughly break the connections,' referring to exposing or disrupting any bribes or influence.
  • 请易之 - 'Please change it,' meaning to replace the candidate.
  • 悉阵抚前 - 'All were arranged before the governor,' indicating the presentation of the exam papers.

  • 主试填注中式，及拆弥封，恰是青阳汪某 - 'The chief examiner marked the candidate as successful, and when the seal was opened, it was exactly the Wang from Qingyang,' indicating the match between the dream and the result.
  • 抚大骇，至公堂上，无不以为大奇 - 'The governor was greatly startled, and in the public hall, no one did not find it miraculous,' indicating the shock and amazement of the officials.
  • 自汪姓一第之后，青阳科甲至今不绝 - 'Since the Wang family passed the examination, Qingyang has never ceased to produce successful candidates,' indicating a lasting impact on the region.

  • 左右欢呼动山谷 - Describes the surrounding area being filled with cheers.
  • 一声鼙鼓震渔阳 - Describes the sound of drums shaking Yuyang.
  • 西幸銮舆道路长 - Describes the emperor's journey westward being long.
  • 娥眉宛转含情死 - Describes a beautiful woman dying with emotion.
  • 马上君王掩面伤 - Describes the emperor covering his face in grief.
  • 炎方仍进青丝笼 - Describes a cage made of green silk being brought from the south.
  • 垂涕还思当日宠 - Describes tears being shed while thinking of past favors.
  • 丹实犹然贡上方 - Describes red fruits still being offered to the imperial court.
  • 朱颜久已归荒冢 - Describes the beauty of a person long since returned to a grave.
  • 妃子妖魂去渺茫 - Describes the spirit of a concubine disappearing into the unknown.
  • 千秋何处识红妆 - Describes the difficulty in recognizing the beauty of a woman after many years.
  • 梦中细说前朝事 - Describes telling stories of the past in a dream.
  • 不及王家十八娘 - Describes the stories not being as interesting as those of a woman named Shiba Nü from the Wang family.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 十八娘 - Refers to a young woman named Shiba Nü, possibly a historical figure or a literary creation.
  • 骊山 - Refers to Lishan Mountain, a famous mountain in Shaanxi Province, known for its historical significance.
  • 长生殿 - Refers to the Hall of Everlasting Life, a palace in the imperial city.
  • 太液池 - Refers to Taiye Pool, a famous imperial garden in Beijing.
  • 梨园 - Refers to the Pear Garden, a place where musicians and actors were trained in ancient China.
  • 鼙鼓 - Refers to a type of drum used in ancient Chinese military and ceremonial contexts.
  • 渔阳 - Refers to Yuyang, a region in northern China, known for its historical significance.
  • 妃子 - Refers to a concubine of the emperor, possibly a reference to Yang Guifei, a famous concubine of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 红妆 - Refers to the makeup and attire of a woman, symbolizing beauty and grace.
  • 王家十八娘 - Refers to a woman named Shiba Nü from the Wang family, possibly a historical figure or a literary creation.

Proper Nouns

  • 陈姬 - A lady named Chen.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 俭，美德也，过则鄙矣 - Classical structure of 'X is a virtue, but excess turns it into something base'; sets up the thesis that moderation is key.
  • 诗剌褊心 - *剌* (censure) indicates moral critique; refers to the *Shijing* (Book of Songs) censuring narrow-mindedness.
  • 不衷于度也 - *衷* means 'to be in the middle' or 'moderate'; *度* means 'measure' or 'norm'; 'does not conform to the proper measure.'
  • 传食糊口 - Common expression for people who subsist on charity or hospitality.
  • 风窗雨屋 - Metaphor for a poor and unstable living condition.
  • 破絮悬鹑 - Classical expression for extreme poverty; *鹑* is a bird used metaphorically for poverty.
  • 泊如也 - 'Calm and untroubled'; *泊* means calm or unattached.
  • 墨沈淋漓 - *沈* refers to ink; *淋漓* means flowing or dripping; 'ink is dripping.'
  • 束身其中 - Refers to being confined in the blanket.
  • 升刑曹主政 - *刑曹* is the judicial department; 'promoted to the head of the Criminal Department.'
  • 车无帏 - *帏* is a curtain or veil; 'the carriage has no curtain.'
  • 高丽纸糊 - *高丽* refers to Goryeo (Korea); *纸糊* is paper used to patch; 'patched with Goryeo paper.'
  • 一骡御之 - *御* means to drive or control; 'driven by one mule.'
  • 五更早起 - *五更* is the fifth watch of the night, just before morning; 'rise before dawn.'
  • 开炕炉 - *炕* is a heated bed or platform; 'light the炕 stove.'
  • 老米饭半锅 - *老米饭* refers to leftover or stale rice; 'half a pot of old rice.'
  • 衙中故有公厨 - *公厨* is a public kitchen; 'the official kitchen was present in the office.'
  • 不肯费 - Refers to refusing to use the official kitchen; 'did not want to spend.'
  • 御车者去城外半日 - *御车者* is the person who drives the carriage; 'the driver went out of the city for half a day.'
  • 为人载 - *载* is to carry or transport; 'to carry people.'
  • 午翘其主人返 - *翘* is likely a typo or miswriting of *归* (return); 'at noon, he would return with the owner.'
  • 其得所钞，可办两日蒭豆 - *钞* is money or currency; *蒭豆* is fodder and grain for animals; 'the money he received could buy two days of fodder and feed.'
  • 捉笔点画律例 - *点画* is to write or draw; 'grab the brush and draw up legal statutes.'
  • 八年成一书 - *成一书* is a common expression for completing a work; 'completed a book in eight years.'
  • 名《律表》 - *律表* is the title of the book; 'named *Lv Biao* (Legal Table).'
  • 亦梓行 - *梓行* is the act of printing using wooden blocks; 'also published in woodblock printing.'
  • 前大学士舒 - *大学士* is Grand Secretary, a high-ranking official; 'the former Grand Secretary Shu.'
  • 以其勤慎，列保荐 - *保荐* is to recommend or nominate; 'because of his diligence and caution, he was recommended.'
  • 蒙恩以繁府用 - *繁府* is a busy or important office; 'was honored with appointment to a busy government office.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 郧阳太守 - Governor of Yunyang, a prefecture in present-day Hubei Province.
  • 国子先生 - Title for a scholar-official at the National Academy (*Guozijian*), the highest educational institution of the time.
  • 壬申 - A year in the Chinese sexagenary cycle, corresponding to a specific year in the calendar.
  • 青《集古滋蕙》帖 - Calligraphy collection titled *Ji Gu Zhi Hui* (Collection of Ancient Calligraphy); *青* refers to blue ink, likely indicating its color or quality.
  • 成均 - Classical term for the National Academy (*Guozijian*), where scholars studied.
  • 律例 - Legal statutes or codes, particularly those of the Qing dynasty.
  • 《律表》 - *Lv Biao*, the title of the book compiled by the author.
  • 梓行 - Refers to woodblock printing, a common method of publishing in classical China.
  • 大学士 - High-ranking official, often a member of the imperial court, equivalent to a Grand Secretary or Chancellor.
  • 繁府 - Term for a busy or important government office, often with a high administrative burden.

Proper Nouns

  • 郧阳太守 - Governor of Yunyang (modern-day Hubei).
  • 省轩 - Name of the person being described; likely a nickname or honorific.
  • 闽籍 - Fujian native or from Fujian province.
  • 国子先生 - National Academy scholar.
  • 壬申 - A year in the Chinese sexagenary cycle.
  • 北皿考学 - Uncertain; possibly a regional or colloquial term for an examination. Marked as uncertain.
  • 《集古滋蕙》帖 - *Ji Gu Zhi Hui* (Collection of Ancient Calligraphy), a calligraphy collection.
  • 成均 - Classical term for the National Academy.
  • 刑曹主政 - Head of the Criminal Department.
  • 己丑 - A year in the Chinese sexagenary cycle.
  • 舒 - Proper name, possibly a former Grand Secretary.
  • 《律表》 - *Lv Biao*, the title of a legal code or table.
  • 大学士 - Grand Secretary, a high-ranking official.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 莅任之日 - The day of taking office.
  • 逾岁 - After a year or over a year.
  • 眷属至 - Family arrived or family came.
  • 其少子年九岁 - The young son who was nine years old.
  • 欲为之购 - Wanted to buy for him or intended to buy for him.
  • 练头 - A type of headgear, possibly a simple cap or hood.
  • 不必冠 - No need for a hat or not necessary to wear a hat.
  • 遂伤脑 - Thus injured the brain or therefore harmed the brain.
  • 以鼻涕死 - Died from nasal discharge or died due to nasal discharge.
  • 其妾，京中人也 - The concubine, who was from the capital city.
  • 足不弓 - Feet are not bound or feet are not bound in the traditional manner.
  • 尝着其破朝靴 - Once wore her old official boots.
  • 敝衣决踵 - Wore tattered clothes or wore ragged clothes.
  • 邋遢而环伺 - Looked untidy and surrounded or looked disheveled and surrounded.
  • 顾而乐之 - Looked at them and was pleased or glanced at them and was delighted.
  • 固不知其背面时 - Did not know when they were behind him or did not realize when they were behind him.
  • 皆狐裘煌煌也 - All were wearing splendid fox fur coats or all were dressed in luxurious fox fur coats.
  • 宴客 - Entertaining guests or hosting guests.
  • 即宴客，亦不饮酒 - Even when entertaining guests, he did not drink alcohol.
  • 回教 - Refers to the Islamic religion.
  • 早契 - Had a long-standing friendship or had a close relationship.
  • 三年之中 - For three years.
  • 不肯到一羊相邀宴 - Refused to attend a banquet invited by one sheep or refused to go to a banquet invited by one sheep.
  • 日昃 - The sun has declined or it was late afternoon.
  • 不得返 - Could not return or was unable to return.
  • 炊饼纳袖 - Carried a steamed bun in his sleeve.
  • 自舆中啖之 - Ate it from the carriage.
  • 逢途中共耳而目之乎 - Did people see and hear me on the road?
  • 我食之，以袖笼口 - I ate it, covering my mouth with my sleeve.
  • 人或见我颊动 - People might see my cheeks moving.
  • 不过谓嚼槟榔、吸鼻烟耳 - They would just think I was chewing betel nuts or sniffing snuff.
  • 初秋 - Refers to early autumn.
  • 蓖麻布袍 - Refers to a coat made of castor bean fabric.
  • 染作米色 - Dyed in a rice color or dyed in a light brown color.
  • 其质有类于羽毛纱 - Its texture is similar to feather gauze or its texture resembles feather gauze.
  • 其色不亚于程乡茧 - Its color is no less than that of Chengxiang silk or its color is comparable to that of Chengxiang silk.
  • 署荆州府 - Refers to serving as the governor of Jingzhou Prefecture.
  • 署有楼 - Refers to the governor's office having a building.
  • 相传有妖物凭之 - It was said that an evil spirit inhabited it.
  • 凡新守至 - Whenever a new governor arrived.
  • 必牲牢音乐以祭 - They must offer sacrifices with livestock and music.
  • 否则祟 - Otherwise, they would be cursed or otherwise, they would suffer misfortune.
  • 省轩不祭 - The governor did not offer sacrifices.
  • 遂病喑 - Thus became mute or therefore fell ill and became mute.
  • 有劝之者，辄摇手，不行 - When someone advised him, he would shake his head and refuse.
  • 至卸篆 - When he resigned from his post.
  • 病亦寻瘥 - His illness soon improved or his illness soon recovered.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 丙申 - The year 1856 in the Gregorian calendar.
  • 湖北郧阳太守 - Refers to the position of governor of Yunyang Prefecture in Hubei Province.
  • 回教 - Refers to the Islamic religion, which was present in China during the Qing dynasty.
  • 程乡茧 - Refers to a type of high-quality silk produced in Chengxiang, known for its fine texture and luster.
  • 署有楼 - Refers to the governor's office building, which was often a significant structure in the local administration.
  • 妖物 - Refers to an evil spirit or supernatural entity believed to inhabit certain places.

[168]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 余过武昌 - I passed through Wuchang (an ancient name for modern-day Wuhan, Hubei province).
  • 与省轩遇 - I met with Shengxuan (the name of the person being addressed).
  • 相留弥月 - We stayed together for a month (弥月 means 'a full month').
  • 每日苦蘖粝餐 - Every day, we had coarse and bitter meals (苦蘖粝餐 describes a simple and unappetizing diet).
  • 不可耐 - Could not bear it (表示无法忍受).
  • 我欲归 - I wanted to return (表示想要回家).
  • 是夜人静 - That night, when it was quiet (表示夜晚安静的时候).
  • 省轩持金二百，置余床头 - Shengxuan held two hundred gold coins and placed them by my bed (表示将钱放在床头).
  • 不腆为叔赆 - A small gift for my uncle (不腆表示谦逊，赆表示送行礼物).
  • 且为祖母寿 - And also as a birthday gift for my grandmother (表示为祖母庆祝生日).
  • 区区饮宴欢聚 - Small gatherings and feasts (表示简单的宴请和聚会).
  • 比处皆然 - It is the same everywhere (表示到处都是如此).
  • 一旦骊驹将驾行者 - When the horse is ready to be driven away (骊驹指代即将启程的人).
  • 不足为一日之春 - Not enough to make one day spring (表示无法带来一天的快乐).
  • 有黯然令人伤心者 - There are those who are sad and heartbroken (表示感到悲伤和心痛).
  • 吾叔以负米计，跋涉千里外 - My uncle, counting on carrying rice, traveled thousands of miles (表示为了生计而长途跋涉).
  • 谅不为餔餟来也 - Surely not for the sake of food (表示不是为了食物而来).
  • 因受其金，且辞其言焉 - I accepted the money and also declined his words (表示接受了礼物但拒绝了他的劝说).
  • 逾年，省轩告归闽 - After a year, Shengxuan informed me he was returning to Fujian (表示一年后省轩要回福建).
  • 年已八十矣 - He was already eighty years old (表示年龄).
  • 噫！俭则固，省轩之谓与 - Ah! Frugality is indeed the hallmark of Shengxuan (表示对省轩节俭的感叹).
  • 然其不为淫祀，不作浪费 - However, he does not engage in excessive rituals or waste (表示不进行过度的祭祀和浪费).
  • 赠远人，安淡泊 - He gives gifts to distant people and finds contentment in simplicity (表示对远方人的赠礼和对简朴生活的满足).
  • 其矫世励俗之行 - His actions to correct the world and inspire the customs (表示他纠正世风和激励风俗的行为).
  • 又当世士大夫中所难能而可贵者也 - And are rare and valuable among the scholars and officials of the world (表示这种行为在当时的士大夫中是难得且宝贵的).
  • 省轩有《俭约》一篇云 - Shengxuan has an essay titled 'Frugality' which says (表示省轩有一篇关于节俭的文章).
  • 盖闻崇俭去奢 - It is said that promoting frugality and eliminating extravagance (表示提倡节俭和消除奢侈).
  • 本属持躬之要 - Is the essence of self-cultivation (表示这是个人修养的关键).
  • 辞华就朴 - Rejecting华丽 and embracing simplicity (表示拒绝华丽和追求朴素).
  • 尤为训俗之宜 - Is especially suitable for teaching and guiding customs (表示特别适合教导和引导风俗).
  • 自世尚虚浮 - Since the world has become superficial (表示社会变得浮躁).
  • 人鲜樽节 - People lack moderation (表示人们缺乏节制).
  • 侈于自奉 - Excessive self-indulgence (表示过度的自我放纵).
  • 争羡何曾之食万钱 - Competing to envy He Zeng's meals costing ten thousand coins (表示羡慕何曾的奢华饮食).
  • 骄以成风 - Pride has become a trend (表示骄傲成为风气).
  • 辄夸孔融之客满座 - Often boasting about Kong Rong's guests filling the room (表示夸耀孔融的宾客满座).
  • 肆筵张乐 - Setting up feasts and music (表示举办宴会和演奏音乐).
  • 笙歌不绝于华堂 - Music and singing never cease in the splendid hall (表示在华丽的大厅中音乐和歌声不断).
  • 开阁延宾 - Opening the gate to welcome guests (表示开门迎接客人).
  • 珍羞日罗于绮席 - Delicacies are daily arranged on the fine table (表示每日在精美的餐桌上摆满佳肴).
  • 虽隆札异数 - Although grand etiquette and special honors (表示虽然有隆重的礼节和特殊的荣誉).
  • 徒费锱铢 - Only waste small amounts of money (表示只是浪费少量的钱财).
  • 而实意真怀 - But the true intentions and genuine feelings (表示真正的意图和真诚的感情).
  • 有何裨益 - Have any benefit (表示有什么好处).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 武昌 - An ancient name for modern-day Wuhan, Hubei province, known for its historical significance.
  • 省轩 - The name of the person being addressed, likely a historical figure or a close friend.
  • 何曾 - A historical figure known for his luxurious lifestyle, often cited as an example of extravagance.
  • 孔融 - A historical figure known for his eloquence and hospitality, often cited as an example of pride and boasting.
  • 《俭约》 - An essay by Shengxuan on the topic of frugality, reflecting his personal values and beliefs.
